quotes: I was asked to teach a construction technology course due to high enrollment and a lack of instructors
there was no curriculum for me to follow. I had to start from scratch
Students passed safety tests in this course, but they did not pass any recognized credentials for the course
in order to add certifications in our district, we had to get approval and funding from CTE supervisors, which wasn't always easy to do
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: Natalie describes building a course from scratch and navigating community partnerships and funding workarounds, which shows some program-level initiative. However, her experience is primarily at the classroom/teacher level rather than the program-coordination or compliance level. She acknowledges she did not pursue recognized credentials for students and was not the one navigating the formal CTE compliance and articulation processes — she was blocked by district-level gatekeepers. There is no mention of articulation agreements, formal Perkins compliance work, or program-level CTE administration. This is below the core bar for a CTE SME role focused on QA and compliance review.

quotes: Looking back, it would be beneficial to have a certification like the NCCER Core in Construction Technology certification
I would first find and review the course standards
I would look and see if there's a student-sponsored association associated with the class and then look for certification tests
I would review the financial requirements of everything that needs to be purchased to ensure that Perkins funding guidelines are in compliance
domain_judgment.rationale: Natalie demonstrates awareness of key CTE domain concepts — standards alignment, student organizations (CTSOs), industry certifications, and Perkins funding — but her knowledge reads as surface-level familiarity rather than deep operational expertise. She names Perkins compliance and NCCER but does not demonstrate how she has actually applied these in a compliance or review context. Her domain vocabulary is present but not substantiated with specific examples of navigating articulation, funding audits, or certification mapping in practice.

quotes: I would first locate and review the suggested curriculum materials associated with the course
I would check to make sure that they're aligned with the standards and take notes of any standards not included
I would make sure that the assignments, assessments, and projects are mapped to the standards
I would evaluate the certification tests to map them to make sure that all the skills on the tests are mapped into the standards and the curriculum
I would review the financial requirements of everything that needs to be purchased to ensure that Perkins funding guidelines are in compliance
I would document all of the gaps clearly and concisely so that the writers can fill in the gaps
process_and_methodology.rationale: For Question 3, Natalie lays out a reasonably structured, multi-step review process: materials review, standards alignment check, assignment/assessment mapping, CTSO integration, certification mapping, Perkins compliance check, and gap documentation. This is a logical and fairly comprehensive sequence. It is not deeply sophisticated — she does not describe a rubric, a gap-analysis template, or how she would prioritize or communicate revision direction — but the process is coherent and covers the major bases expected for a CTE course review.

quotes: Creating the lectures for this course wasn't the hard part. It was helping students make real-world connections with the content that really challenged me
One pathway I know deeply is arts, audio, visual technology, and communications. This pathway encompasses all the ways that humans communicate with each other
I would first locate and review the suggested curriculum materials associated with the course. And once I found those, I would check to make sure that they're aligned with the standards
communication.rationale: Natalie communicates clearly and in an organized manner. Her answers follow a logical sequence and she uses plain language effectively. Question 1 is somewhat narrative and anecdote-heavy, which dilutes the professional focus, but Questions 2 and 3 are more structured and on-point. She is easy to follow throughout and does not ramble excessively. Communication is solid but not exceptional — she does not use precise professional terminology consistently or demonstrate particularly crisp synthesis.

quotes: This video captures my responses to the three questions provided
Question one...Second question...Question three
Thank you so much for this opportunity and I hope to hear from you soon
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: Natalie explicitly labels and answers all three questions in order. Her delivery is professional, warm, and appropriately concise. She opens with a self-introduction and closes with a courteous sign-off. No hard-fail conditions apply on this dimension.

recommendation_reason: Total score 14 meets the threshold (>=14) but Role Fit 2 does not meet the gating criterion (>=3).
overall_summary: Natalie Emery is a classroom-level CTE teacher who built a construction technology course from scratch and shows genuine initiative in community partnerships and resourcefulness. However, her background does not clearly meet the program-level CTE coordination and compliance bar required for this SME role. She is aware of key CTE concepts (Perkins, NCCER, CTSOs, articulation) but does not demonstrate hands-on experience navigating these systems in a compliance or QA capacity. Her review methodology in Question 3 is structured and covers the right bases, making her a reasonable candidate for a content-review support role, but her domain expertise depth is a meaningful gap for a senior SME position. No hard-fail conditions apply. Confidence is high given a complete, clear transcript.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
